perturbation.original_text: 1.21 "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" means, with respect to the efforts to be expended by a Party with respect to any objective (e.g., Development Activities and Commercialization hereunder), the level of efforts consistent with the efforts and resources [***] of similar market potential, at a similar stage in development or product lifecycle, taking into account the stage of development or product lifecycle of other of [***] product candidates, safety and efficacy, product profile, cost of goods, the competitiveness of the marketplace, such company's patent position with respect to such product (including such company's ability to obtain or enforce, or have obtained or enforced, such patent rights), the Third Party patent landscape relevant to the product, the regulatory structure involved, the likelihood of regulatory approval, the likelihood and extent of anticipated or actual profitability of the applicable product, and other technical, legal, scientific and medical considerations. Without limiting the foregoing, Commercially Reasonable Efforts requires, with respect to such obligations, that a Party: (i) promptly assign responsibility for such obligation to specific employee(s) who are held accountable for progress and monitor such progress on an on-going basis, (ii) set objectives for carrying out such obligations, and (iii) allocate resources designed to advance progress with respect to such objectives.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.21 "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" means, with respect to the efforts to be expended by a Party with respect to any objective (e.g., Development Activities and Commercialization hereunder), the level of efforts the party deems appropriate. This may include, but is not limited to, minimal effort, or no effort at all. The party has complete discretion to decide the level of effort required. Without limiting the foregoing, Commercially Reasonable Efforts requires, with respect to such obligations, that a Party: (i) assign responsibility for such obligation to any employee(s), (ii) set objectives as it sees fit for carrying out such obligations, and (iii) allocate resources as it sees fit to advance progress with respect to such objectives.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' allows a party to expend minimal or no effort, which directly contradicts the established legal interpretation. It eliminates the requirement to consider factors like market potential, safety, efficacy, and profitability, creating a loophole for non-performance. This is a legal contradiction because it deviates from the accepted meaning requiring a standard of effort consistent with a reasonable business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition permits a party to act in bad faith by exerting minimal effort. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in all contracts under UCC § 1-304, which requires honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, a principle codified in UCC § 1-304. By allowing a party to exert 'minimal effort, or no effort at all,' the changed text enables actions that are not honest and do not observe reasonable commercial standards, directly conflicting with the obligation of good faith required by the UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The altered definition of 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' effectively negates this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 1.32 "Force Majeure" means any circumstances whatsoever which are not within the reasonable control of the Party affected thereby, potentially including an act of God, war, act of terrorism, insurrection, riot, strike or labor dispute, shortage of materials, fire, explosion, flood, earthquake, government requisition or allocation, breakdown of or damage to plant, equipment or facilities, interruption or delay in transportation, fuel supplies or electrical power, embargo, boycott, order or act of civil or military authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.32 "Force Majeure" means any circumstances whatsoever, including circumstances within the reasonable control of the Party affected thereby, potentially including normal business risks, changes in market conditions, or economic downturns. The Party affected by any circumstance, whether within or outside of its control, shall be excused from performance.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition specifies events 'not within the reasonable control' of the party. The modified text includes circumstances *within* the party's control, negating the purpose of Force Majeure. Further, including 'normal business risks' and 'economic downturns' contradicts the legal understanding of Force Majeure, which typically requires unforeseeable and external events.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 79 of the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 79 of the CISG and the common law understanding of force majeure require an impediment beyond a party's control. By including circumstances within a party's control and standard business risks, the modified definition directly contradicts this fundamental requirement, making the clause potentially unenforceable under international and domestic contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: test Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). How to search - Contact - Team - Trustees - Disclaimer / Legal information / Privacy - Twitter - Facebook A project of CENTRAL , University of Cologne.
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the definition of "Force Majeure" to include circumstances within a party's control and normal business risks, which directly contradicts the common legal understanding and Article 79 of the CISG, as explained in the Law Explanation. While the provided scraped law snippets are not directly applicable, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the original definition specifies events 'not within the reasonable control' of the party, and the modified text negates this purpose by including circumstances within the party's control. The inclusion of 'normal business risks' further undermines the legal understanding of Force Majeure, which requires unforeseeable and external events. Therefore, the modification introduces a significant and clear legal contradiction, justifying a 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: 10.5 No Other Representations or Warranties. EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY STATED IN THIS AGREEMENT, NO REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, NON-INFRINGEMENT, OR NON-MISAPPROPRIATION OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, ARE MADE OR GIVEN BY OR ON BEHALF OF A PARTY. EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY STATED IN THIS AGREEMENT, ALL REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES, WHETHER ARISING BY OPERATION OF LAW OR OTHERWISE, ARE HEREBY EXPRESSLY EXCLUDED.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.5 No Other Representations or Warranties. ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES, INCLUDING WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ARE HEREBY INCLUDED. However, there are no express warranties made.
perturbation.explanation: The original text disclaims all implied warranties. The modified text states that all implied warranties *are* included, which is a direct contradiction. This creates uncertainty regarding the quality and suitability of the product, especially concerning 'merchantability' and 'fitness for a particular purpose'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - § 2-316: Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: While UCC § 2-316 allows for the exclusion or modification of warranties, the original clause effectively disclaims them. By inverting the meaning and explicitly including implied warranties, the edited clause undermines the intended disclaimer. However, also specifying that there are no express warranties can also cause further ambiguity if there are items in the agreement that could be seen as express warranties (rendering the agreement unclear).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent to disclaim implied warranties. The original text explicitly excludes all representations and warranties, including implied warranties. The changed text states the opposite, that 'ALL implied warranties...ARE HEREBY INCLUDED'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-316, allows for the exclusion or modification of warranties, but the change here doesn't just modify; it inverts the meaning entirely, moving from disclaimer to inclusion, therefore creating a legal contradiction.
